content.problemStatement:
A mother bought a set of NNN toys for her 222 kids, Alice and Bob. She has already decided which toy goes to whom, however she has forgotten the monetary values of the toys. She only remembers that she ordered the toys in ascending order of their value. The prices are always non-negative.

A distribution is said to be fair when no matter what the actual values were, the difference between the values of the toys Alice got, and the toys Bob got, does not exceed the maximum value of any toy.

Formally, let viv_ivi​ be the value of iii-th toy, and SSS be a binary string such that Si=1S_i = 1Si​=1 if the toy is to be given to Alice, and Si=0S_i = 0Si​=0 if the toy is to be given to Bob.

Then, the distribution represented by SSS is said to be fair if, for **all possible** arrays vvv satisfying 0≤v1≤v2≤....≤vN0 \le v_1 \le v_2 \le .... \le v_N0≤v1​≤v2​≤....≤vN​,

∣∑i=1Nvi⋅[si=1]−∑i=1Nvi⋅[si=0]∣≤vN\left| \sum\limits_{i = 1}^{N} v_i \cdot [s_i = 1] - \sum\limits_{i = 1}^{N} v_i \cdot [s_i = 0] \right| \leq v_N​i=1∑N​vi​⋅[si​=1]−i=1∑N​vi​⋅[si​=0]​≤vN​where [P][P][P] is 111 iff PPP is true, and 000 otherwise.

You are given the binary string SSS representing the distribution.

Print `YES` if the given distribution is fair, and `NO` otherwise.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, the number of toys.
The second line of each test case contains a binary string SSS of length NNN.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: `YES` or `NO` depending on whether SSS represents a fair distribution or not.


Each character of the output may be printed in either lowercase or uppercase, i.e, the strings `NO`, `no`, `nO`, and `No` will all be treated as equivalent.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 3·10^5
S consists only of characters '0' and '1'
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N (the number of toys), the second line contains a binary string S of length N describing the distribution (S_i = '1' means the i‑th toy goes to Alice, '0' means it goes to Bob).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if the given distribution is fair, otherwise "NO".

sampleTestCases.output:
YES
NO
YES
NO
YES
NO
sampleTestCases.explanation: Each test case is evaluated by checking every suffix of the binary string. For example, in the fourth test case (S = "1100") the whole string has three 1's and one 0, difference = 2 > 1, so the answer is NO.

solution.approach:
Let a_i = +1 if S_i = '1' (toy for Alice) and a_i = -1 otherwise. For any non‑decreasing non‑negative values v_1 ≤ … ≤ v_N, the worst case for the absolute difference D = Σ a_i·v_i is obtained by setting the first k toys to value 0 and the remaining N‑k toys to a large value X. Then D = X·Σ_{i>k} a_i. Because X can be arbitrarily large, the condition |D| ≤ v_N (=X) holds for all possible v only if for every suffix the sum Σ_{i>k} a_i is in the set {‑1,0,1}. In other words, for every suffix the numbers of 1’s and 0’s differ by at most one.

Algorithm per test case:
1. Initialise balance = 0.
2. Scan the string from right to left.
   - If current character is '1' add +1 to balance, else add -1.
   - After the update, if balance < -1 or balance > 1, the distribution is not fair → print "NO" and stop processing this test case.
3. If the whole scan finishes without violation, print "YES".
The scan is O(N) time and O(1) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, total O(Σ N) ≤ 3·10^5.
